🌟 Project overview

What this robot does

The project focuses on an affordable human-following robot that detects a nearby person using ultrasonic sensing, follows at walking pace, stops for obstacles, toggles modes using sound-based activation, and accepts manual Bluetooth override when required.

🎯 Problem statement

Conventional small robots often depend on remote control or fixed paths, which makes them less useful in dynamic spaces. This project addresses the need for a hands-free robot that can move with a human target in real time and support assistance use cases in healthcare, warehouses, elderly care, and personal transport.

Hands-free assistance Dynamic indoor movement Low-cost embedded design Autonomous + manual hybrid control

✅ Core objectives

1
Autonomous following
Detect and follow a human target using ultrasonic sensing.
2
Safe motion
Use IR-based obstacle detection to stop or avoid collisions.
3
Hands-free activation
Toggle active/standby mode through clap-based sound detection.
4
Wireless override
Allow HC-05 Bluetooth control through a mobile app.
5
Battery-backed operation
Run on a rechargeable 18650 system for at least 1.5 hours.

🛠️ System design

Architecture, hardware stack & motion logic

The robot is organized as sensing, control, actuation, communication, and power subsystems centered around the Arduino UNO R3. Each subsystem can be tested independently before final integration.

📡

Sensing subsystem

HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor measures target distance. IR sensor provides close-range obstacle detection. Sound sensor supports clap-based mode toggling.

🧠

Control subsystem

Arduino UNO R3 with ATmega328P reads sensors, applies following logic, manages states, and sends PWM plus direction signals to the motor driver.

⚙️

Actuation subsystem

Four DC motors on a 4WD acrylic chassis are driven through the L298N dual H-bridge for forward, backward, left, right, and stop actions.

📱

Communication subsystem

HC-05 Bluetooth module enables manual mobile commands and override control when autonomous mode needs intervention.

🔋

Power subsystem

Rechargeable 18650 Li-Ion battery with TP4056 charging and protection module. Single-battery architecture keeps the build compact and practical.

🔊

Feedback subsystem

Active buzzer gives audible feedback for activation, deactivation, reverse movement, and obstacle alerts, improving user awareness during operation.

🧭 Operating states

0

Standby

Robot is powered on but inactive. Motors are stopped. It waits for a double-clap or Bluetooth command.

1

Following

Robot tracks the nearest object ahead and adjusts speed according to distance bands. Obstacle detection stays active.

2

Obstacle stop

If an obstacle is detected inside the IR range, all motors stop and the buzzer signals until the path clears.

3

Bluetooth override

Manual movement commands suspend autonomous behavior until an A command or timeout returns the robot to following mode.

⚡ Speed control logic

> 100 cmPWM 255

Target is too far ahead → full speed forward

60–100 cmPWM 200

Closing the gap at high speed

40–60 cmPWM 150

Medium speed near target zone

20–40 cmPWM 90

Ideal safe following range

< 20 cmPWM 0

Too close → stop and wait

🧩 Main components

ComponentRoleKey spec
Arduino UNO R3Main controllerATmega328P, 5V, 16 MHz
HC-SR04Human distance detection2–400 cm range, approx. ±3 mm to ±3 cm practical class
IR obstacle sensorCollision prevention2–30 cm range, digital output
Sound sensorDouble-clap activationAdjustable threshold
HC-05 BluetoothWireless manual override~10 m range, 3.3V logic RX
L298N motor driver4-motor directional driveDual H-bridge, 2A/channel
18650 battery + TP4056Rechargeable power3.7V nominal, protection charging

🎮 Manual command set

F
Forward
Moves robot forward at fixed override speed.
B
Backward
Moves backward with buzzer indication.
L
Left
Turns left by differential motor action.
R
Right
Turns right by differential motor action.
S
Stop
Stops all motors immediately.
A
Auto mode
Returns control to autonomous following logic.

📍 Pin mapping used in the appended Arduino code

🧷 Practical wiring map

Arduino pinConnected itemPurpose
D2IR RightRight-side obstacle / directional sensing
D3IR LeftLeft-side obstacle / directional sensing
D4HC-05 RXSoftware serial receive path
D5L298N ENALeft motor speed PWM
D6L298N ENBRight motor speed PWM
D7, D8L298N IN1, IN2Left motor direction
D9, D10L298N IN3, IN4Right motor direction
D11, D12HC-SR04 TRIG, ECHOUltrasonic distance measurement
D13HC-05 TXSoftware serial transmit path
A0Mode buttonManual mode toggle
A1BuzzerAudio feedback and alerts

The HC-05 RX line should be protected using a resistor divider because the Bluetooth module expects 3.3V logic on its RX pin.

🧪 Testing & validation

Measured performance, findings & build observations

The prototype was tested across distance sensing, motion control, obstacle response, clap activation, Bluetooth override, and endurance. One surface-related case was only partially passed and then mitigated in software.

📏

Ultrasonic accuracy

Measured error remained within about ±1–3 cm from 10 cm to 200 cm, which is sufficient for the selected following zone.

🔋

Battery performance

The robot exceeded the 1.5-hour target with a best recorded continuous following runtime of 1 hour 50 minutes.

✅

Overall test result

11 out of 12 test cases passed. Carpet surface following achieved partial pass and was mitigated using a software PWM increase.

📊 Performance tables

Actual distanceMeasured 1Measured 2Error
10 cm10 cm10 cm0 cm
20 cm20 cm21 cm±1 cm
30 cm31 cm30 cm±1 cm
50 cm51 cm50 cm±1 cm
80 cm81 cm80 cm±1 cm
100 cm100 cm102 cm±2 cm
150 cm151 cm149 cm±2 cm
200 cm203 cm201 cm±3 cm
ModeRuntimeEnd voltage
Continuous following at 50% average PWM1h 50m3.54V
Continuous following at 75% average PWM1h 22m3.51V
Bluetooth override constant movement1h 05m3.48V
Standby / idle6h 30m3.60V

🛠️ Build observations & corrective actions

Left motors initially reversed
Swapped IN1 / IN2 connections on L298N Channel A to fix wrong polarity.
Sound sensor false triggering from motor noise
Added 0.1 μF decoupling capacitor and increased debounce delay to 100 ms.
HC-SR04 unstable near low battery voltage
Added a 100 μF electrolytic capacitor near the 5V and GND rails for stability.
Robot drifted left during straight-line following
Applied a +10 PWM offset on the left channel to compensate motor mismatch.
Bluetooth pairing initially unstable
Reconfigured HC-05 to 9600 baud using AT command mode.

🧾 Features tested

Distance measurement Obstacle stop Clap detection PWM direction control Bluetooth override Full integration behavior Battery endurance Carpet surface calibration

🧠 Inference

The report concludes that ultrasonic sensing offers a reliable and budget-appropriate method for short-range human following without the complexity of camera-based systems. Combined with an IR safety layer and Bluetooth override, the robot demonstrates practical viability as a diploma-level embedded robotics system.

📅 Planning & management

Milestones, WBS, timeline, cost & risks

The project was planned across a 16-week semester timeline with formal internal checkpoints and tightly controlled cost, documentation, and integration stages.

🗺️ Milestone roadmap

W4

Scope & planning completed

Scope document, preliminary research, WBS, CBS, timeline, and first evaluation checkpoint.

W5

Procurement & schematic design

Components were sourced and the circuit was created using Fritzing-style planning.

W8

Assembly & code development

Hardware assembled, ultrasonic logic created, Bluetooth integration tested.

W12

Validation & debugging

Sensor calibration, full integration, results analysis, and third evaluation checkpoint.

W16

Final report & SEE demo

Project report, presentation deck, and working model demonstration completed.

📝

Daily log sample

Kick-off, WBS/CBS work, literature survey, circuit design, assembly, code, integration, results documentation, report writing, and SEE preparation were tracked weekly.

⚠️

Risk register

Major risks included false sensor readings, battery drain, Bluetooth range issues, code logic errors, and sound sensor false activations.

🤝

Role distribution

Adithya led code development; Mayur handled documentation and reporting; Darshan and Anjaneya focused on development and integration support.

📚 Expanded risk analysis ＋
Sensor malfunction or false readings
Mitigation: test sensors individually and keep a spare HC-SR04.
Battery drains faster than expected
Mitigation: efficient PWM control and keep a spare charged 18650 cell.
Bluetooth range limitation indoors
Mitigation: limit Bluetooth to override only, not core following logic.
Robot cannot distinguish human from other objects
Mitigation: use a fixed front-facing sensor orientation and tuned thresholds.
Code logic errors
Mitigation: incremental testing plus Arduino Serial Monitor debugging.
Motor driver overheating
Mitigation: maintain airflow and stay within current ratings.
📈 Business & future scope

Applications, value, strengths, limitations & outlook

Beyond the academic prototype, the concept can evolve into practical assistive and industrial platforms that reduce physical load, improve operational efficiency, and provide hands-free transport support.

💎 Value proposition

The system offers hands-free real-time following without requiring predefined routes or continuous manual remote control. That means lower cognitive effort for the user, practical transport assistance, and a clear path toward richer assistive robotics use cases.

Application areaUse caseBenefit
Healthcare & elderly careAssist patients and carry personal itemsReduces physical strain on caregivers
Logistics & warehousingFollow workers with tools or goodsIncreases efficiency and reduces fatigue
Retail & shoppingSmart carts following customersImproves customer experience
Tourism & hospitalityLuggage-carrying robotCreates premium guest interaction
Construction & industryTool transport assistantReduces repetitive carrying tasks
Education & researchLab movement assistantAutomates routine movement support

⚖️ Advantages vs limitations

👍 Advantages

  • Autonomous mobility without constant manual control
  • Hands-free transport support for users
  • Affordable embedded implementation for education
  • Useful hybrid control with Bluetooth override
  • Expandable architecture for future features

👀 Limitations

  • Ultrasonic beam is narrow and may lose fast side movement
  • Cannot differentiate a human from another object at same distance
  • Reflective indoor surfaces can introduce false echoes
  • Battery duration still limits long sessions
  • Crowded environments remain challenging without vision
Why ultrasonic? 💸 Low cost Why not vision? 🧮 Too complex for budget Why Bluetooth? 🎮 Manual safety override
🤖

Future upgrade

Add computer vision for target identification and better crowd handling.

🌐

Future upgrade

Introduce IoT connectivity, telemetry, and remote monitoring dashboards.

🗣️

Future upgrade

Add conversational interaction and richer human-robot communication layers.

📘 Technical appendix

Code summary, glossary, references & learning outcomes

This section condenses the appendices from the report so the website still feels rich, complete, and presentation-ready without becoming hard to read on smaller screens.

💻 Arduino code overview

/* Bluetooth */
SoftwareSerial BT(13, 4);   // TX, RX
bool autoMode = true;

/* Button & Buzzer */
#define MODE_BTN A0
#define BUZZER   A1

/* Ultrasonic */
#define TRIG_PIN 11
#define ECHO_PIN 12
#define MAX_DISTANCE 200
#define MIN_DIST 7
#define MAX_DIST 30

/* IR Sensors */
#define IR_RIGHT 2
#define IR_LEFT  3

/* L298N */
#define ENA 5
#define IN1 7
#define IN2 8
#define ENB 6
#define IN3 9
#define IN4 10

void loop(){
  if(!autoMode && BT.available()){
    char cmd = BT.read();
    // F, B, L, R, S commands
  }
  if(autoMode){
    followMe();
  }
}

🧪 Why the algorithm works

1
Distance sensing
The ultrasonic module measures distance continuously and updates the control loop.
2
Decision logic
The robot chooses stop / slow / medium / fast movement according to the measured range.
3
Safety gating
IR obstacle detection can immediately interrupt movement regardless of follow state.
4
Manual override
Bluetooth commands temporarily replace autonomous decisions when manual action is needed.

🧾 Conclusion from the report ＋
The Human Following Robot was successfully designed, built, programmed, tested, and documented within the semester timeline and a total hardware budget of ₹1,417. The report states that the system reliably followed a person, avoided obstacles, responded to clap activation, and accepted Bluetooth override commands. The platform provides a strong base for future extensions such as computer vision, IoT connectivity, and smarter social navigation.
💬 Project answering AI

Ask questions about the project right inside the website

This page now includes an offline project answering assistant. It is designed to answer common viva, demo, and presentation questions directly from the report content, so the website becomes useful during review, rehearsal, and demonstration.

🤖 What the assistant can answer

What is the objective? Which sensors are used? What is the cost? What is the battery backup? Why ultrasonic instead of camera? What are the Bluetooth commands? What tests were performed? Who are the team members?

Use the floating 💬 button at the bottom-right corner to open it from anywhere on the page.
